prompt:
SRE platform ticket. We manage a few hundred Datadog monitors from a git repo, and the current "sync" job is a Bash loop over curl that GETs one page of monitors, PUTs whole monitor JSON blobs back (stomping options set in the UI and once wiping restricted_roles by sending null — that opened a locked monitor to the whole org), and gets itself 429'd every Monday. Replace it with a Go package `ddmon` (stdlib only, package name ddmon at the module root) that inventories monitors and reconciles only the fields we own. ddmon_test.go is the acceptance contract.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the current official Datadog documentation and official client sources — at minimum the Datadog API reference for monitors (which operations are v1 vs what the v2 monitor namespace actually contains — pick the version intentionally and say why), the exact list/edit operation contracts including every pagination control on the list call and the auth headers, the monitor object's readonly vs editable fields including restricted_roles semantics, and Datadog's rate-limit response headers. Datadog's official API client repositories on GitHub (DataDog org) count as first-party sources; blogs and Stack Overflow do not. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live docs against them — if anything disagrees, report the conflict explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests use:
- NewClient(Config{BaseURL, APIKey, AppKey, PageSize, MaxRetries, Sleep, HTTPClient}) — both documented auth headers on every request, never credentials in the query string; PageSize defaults to the official pager's page size; Sleep is injected (never time.Sleep directly).
- ListMonitors(ctx, Filter{Name, MonitorTags}) ([]Monitor, error) — pages through the documented monitor list operation with the documented page controls until a short page, comma-joins MonitorTags, omits empty filter params, and decodes id/name/type/query/message/tags/priority (nullable)/restricted_roles/options — options retained as raw JSON so unknown keys survive round-trips.
- Reconcile(ctx, filter, []Desired{Name, Query, Message, Tags, Priority}) (Report, error) — matches desired monitors to inventory by name. Owned fields are query, message, tags, priority: tags compare order-insensitively, a nil desired Priority means unmanaged (neither compared nor sent). Drift triggers ONE update via the documented edit operation whose body carries only the owned fields plus the monitor's current options object and restricted_roles list echoed verbatim — an unrestricted monitor's restricted_roles key must be omitted entirely (a JSON null would open editing to everyone), and readonly fields must never be sent. No drift means no write at all. Report{Updated, Unchanged, Missing, Failed} in desired order; a per-monitor API failure (decoded from Datadog's errors envelope, credentials never included) is recorded in Failed and must not stop the remaining monitors.
- Rate limits: when a response reports the documented remaining-requests header as 0, wait the documented reset header's seconds (via Sleep) before the next request; on 429 honor Retry-After first, then the reset header, then 1s, retrying the same request up to MaxRetries times before recording the failure.

Acceptance: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... — it runs a loopback fake Datadog API; no real Datadog, no real credentials. ddmon_test.go, go.mod, and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

official_sources.title: datadog-api-client-go api/datadogV1/api_monitors.go (official Datadog client source)
official_sources.used_for: exact operations: GET /api/v1/monitor with query params group_states, name, tags, monitor_tags, with_downtimes, id_offset, page, page_size; PUT /api/v1/monitor/{monitor_id}; auth header names DD-API-KEY and DD-APPLICATION-KEY; the official ListMonitorsWithPagination helper advances the page parameter with a default page_size of 100 until a page returns fewer results than page_size

official_sources.title: datadog-api-client-go api/datadogV1/model_monitor.go (official Datadog client source)
official_sources.used_for: monitor object fields and JSON tags; readonly fields (id, created, creator, modified, overall_state, matching_downtimes); restricted_roles as the list of role identifiers allowed to edit the monitor; priority 1 (high) to 5 (low)

official_sources.title: datadog-api-client-go api/datadogV1/model_monitor_update_request.go (official Datadog client source)
official_sources.used_for: MonitorUpdateRequest: every field optional (partial update body), including options, restricted_roles (nullable list), priority (nullable int), tags, query, message

official_sources.title: How to set up RBAC for Monitors — Datadog Docs
official_sources.used_for: restricted_roles semantics on create/edit: role UUIDs restrict who can edit; setting restricted_roles to null allows editing for all users with Monitor Write permission — so an update must never emit null accidentally

official_sources.title: Rate Limits — Datadog API Reference
official_sources.used_for: X-RateLimit-Limit/Period/Remaining/Reset/Name response headers, their meanings, and 429 retry guidance

official_sources.title: Monitors — Datadog API Reference
official_sources.used_for: current operation inventory: monitor CRUD and pagination live under /api/v1/monitor; the v2 monitor namespace covers only configuration policies, notification rules, and monitor templates

research.language_rationale: The fleet tooling this reconciler joins is Go. Datadog's official datadog-api-client-go is a generated wrapper over these same v1 endpoints, and the verify environment is offline and stdlib-only, so net/http against the documented v1 monitor operations is the credible integration surface; the client source doubles as the authoritative wire reference.
